one_line_takeaway: A purely-visual, multi-example contrastive loss plus heavy surrogate ensembling and data augmentation turns open-source-crafted image perturbations into highly transferable TARGETED attacks on proprietary VLLMs (over 94% ASR on GPT-4o), while Claude proves substantially more robust.
key_finding: Targeted image perturbations crafted purely on open-source surrogates transfer strongly to proprietary VLLMs. On image captioning at epsilon=16/255 (using only 8 CLIP surrogates) the attack reaches 94.4% ASR on GPT-4o and 96.1% on GPT-4o mini, versus a cited prior baseline (Dong et al. 2023) that achieved only 45% UNtargeted ASR on GPT-4V. Two design choices dominate transferability: image (visual) examples beat text examples (94.4% vs 82.0% on GPT-4o at N-visual=50 vs N-text=1) and scaling to a sufficient number of CLIP surrogates matters more than adding VLLM/visual-only/adv-trained surrogates. Claude models are markedly more robust than GPT-4o/Gemini, especially at small epsilon (Claude 3.5 Sonnet only 15.1% at epsilon=8/255 vs GPT-4o 83.9%).
core_contribution: The paper introduces a transfer-based, TARGETED adversarial attack that crafts image perturbations on an ensemble of open-source surrogate models and transfers them to proprietary black-box VLLMs (GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini) to induce attacker-chosen MISINTERPRETATIONS of visual inputs (not toxic-text jailbreaks). The technical novelty is a 'Visual Contrastive Loss' (Eq. 7) that uses ONLY the surrogate's visual encoder with N=50 positive and N=50 negative IMAGE examples (top-K=10 for positives), replacing the standard single-positive/single-negative image-TEXT CLIP similarity loss (Eq. 3, attributed to prior work by Wu et al.). This is combined with model-level tricks (large CLIP-model ensembles plus DropPath, PatchDrop, and perturbation moving-averaging) and a data-augmentation pipeline (Gaussian noise, random crop/pad/resize, differentiable random JPEGify). The authors emphasize that the attack mechanism follows the same principles as prior image-only/text-only transfer work, but the choice of a purely-visual, multi-example contrastive OBJECTIVE is what drives the large jump in targeted transfer success.
threat_model: Black-box, transfer-based, targeted evasion. The attacker has NO access to the victim proprietary VLLM's weights, gradients, or preprocessing pipeline; only query/output access. The attacker has full white-box access to a set of open-source surrogate models (CLIP variants, open VLLMs, visual-only encoders). Perturbations are L-infinity norm-bounded (epsilon = 8/255, 16/255, 32/255 tested). Goal: make the victim produce a response semantically aligned with an attacker-chosen target (misclassify an object, misinterpret one image as another in VQA, or misread receipt text), e.g. reading hazardous content as safe or overlooking restricted material. A 'universal' variant crafts one perturbation applicable across many images/models (claimed in intro/abstract; detailed universal tables are deferred to the appendix, not in the extracted body).
limitations: Text-recognition (receipt) attacks are much weaker and need a large, visually NOTICEABLE perturbation (epsilon up to 32/255), reaching only 52.5% ASR on GPT-4o and 32.5% on Claude 3.5; the authors concede results 'remain visually noticeable.' Evaluation scale is small (50 captioning images, 30 VQA images, 20 receipt images). Success is graded largely by LLM judges (GPT-4o/GPT-4) which may bias the metric. Claude's robustness and low VQA detail/reasoning ASR show the attack is not uniformly effective. No defenses are proposed or evaluated. Detailed 'universal perturbation' quantitative tables are relegated to the appendix and not in the main experimental body. Victim model versions are pinned to specific 2024-2025 releases.

datasets_used: NIPS 2017 Adversarial Learning Challenges dev set (1000 images; 50 selected for image captioning)
ImageNet-1K / ImageNet-12K (source of positive/negative class examples)
LAION-5B (keyword retrieval when target category absent from ImageNet)
LLaVA-Bench (COCO) (30 images x conversation/detail/reasoning questions, VQA setting)
Retail receipt OCR dataset - TrainingDataPro/Kaggle (20 receipt images, text-recognition setting)
quantitative_results: Image captioning ASR at eps=16/255 (8 CLIP surrogates): GPT-4o 94.4%, GPT-4o mini 96.1%, Gemini 1.5 Pro 86.1%, Llama-3.2 90B 91.4%, Qwen2-VL 7B 89.9%, Claude 3.5 Sonnet 58.7%, Claude 3.7 Sonnet 62.7%
Image captioning ASR at eps=8/255: GPT-4o 83.9%, GPT-4o mini 84.8%, Gemini 1.5 Pro 70.2%, Claude 3.5 Sonnet 15.1%, Claude 3.7 Sonnet 21.3%
Cited baseline contrast: prior Dong et al. 2023 = 45% UNtargeted ASR on GPT-4V vs this paper >95% TARGETED ASR on GPT-4o

Surrogate ablation (eps=16/255): 3 CLIP -> GPT-4o 85.1% / Claude 3.5 36.3%; 8 CLIP -> GPT-4o 94.4% / Claude 3.5 58.7%; adding VLLM/visual-only/adv surrogates to 8 CLIP gives little gain (93.4-94.8% GPT-4o)
Loss ablation (eps=16/255): textual N=1 -> GPT-4o 82.0% / Claude 46.8%; visual N=50,K=10 -> GPT-4o 94.4% / Claude 58.7%; visual N=50,K=50 drops to GPT-4o 87.0%
Augmentation breakdown (Claude 3.5, eps=16/255): full 58.7%, no DropPath 42.4%, no Random Pad 46.7% (GPT-4o largely unaffected, ~93-95%)
VQA ASR at eps=16/255 (conversation/detail/reasoning): GPT-4o 57.8/21.1/93.3, Claude 3.5 26.7/11.1/41.1, Gemini 1.5 Pro 42.2/25.6/57.7
Sanity check: random perturbation at eps=16/255 yields near-zero ASR on GPT-4o and Claude 3.5
baselines_compared: Standard single positive/negative CLIP image-TEXT similarity loss (Eq. 3, attributed to Wu et al.) vs proposed Visual Contrastive Loss (Eq. 7)
Dong et al. 2023 (untargeted attack on GPT-4V/Bard) cited as prior transfer benchmark
Surrogate-composition baselines: 3 CLIP vs 8 CLIP vs 8 CLIP + VLLMs/visual-only/adversarially-trained models
Loss hyperparameter baselines: textual vs visual examples, varying N (1/10/20/50) and K (10/50)
Ablations toggling DropPath, PatchDrop, perturbation averaging, random crop/resize, random pad, random JPEGify
